visual_checks: Look for session timeout warnings, countdown timers, or 'your session will expire' notifications visible on the page or in the screenshot sequence
Check for auto-advancing content such as rotating carousels with a visible timer, news tickers, or slideshow countdowns
Look for timed form submissions — indicators like 'You have X minutes to complete this form' or countdown displays
Identify auto-refreshing content areas or pages with meta refresh indicators
Look for auction-style or real-time bidding interfaces with time-limited actions
Check for any visible mechanism to extend, adjust, or turn off time limits (e.g., 'Need more time?' link, timeout settings, pause button on auto-advancing content)
Look for login pages or authenticated areas that might enforce session timeouts
Check for timed assessments, quizzes, or surveys with visible timer elements
pass_conditions: No time limits are imposed on user interaction with the page content
Time limits that exist provide a mechanism to turn off the time limit before it starts
Time limits that exist allow the user to adjust the duration to at least 10 times the default

Time limits that exist warn the user at least 20 seconds before expiration and allow extending with a simple action (e.g., pressing a key or clicking a button), and this extension can be done at least 10 times
The time limit is part of a real-time event (live auction, live broadcast) where no alternative to the time limit is possible
The time limit is essential (timed exam where timing is a core requirement) and no alternative exists
fail_conditions: A session timeout occurs with no prior warning and no option to extend
A countdown timer enforces a time limit with no mechanism to turn it off, adjust it, or extend it
Auto-advancing content (carousel, slideshow, rotating banner) moves on a timer with no pause, stop, or speed adjustment control
A form has a time limit that causes data loss when exceeded, with no warning or extension option
The page auto-refreshes or redirects after a set period with no way to prevent or delay the refresh
A session timeout warning appears but provides fewer than 20 seconds to respond, or extension is limited to fewer than 10 times
na_conditions: Page has no time-limited interactions — content is static with no timers, auto-refresh, auto-advance, or session timeout mechanisms
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots and tab walk data cannot reveal server-side session timeouts or JavaScript timers not yet triggered during the test
The testing window was too short to observe time-limited behavior that may occur after a longer period
common_mistakes: Session timeouts that silently redirect to a login page without warning, losing unsaved form data
Carousel or slideshow auto-rotation with no visible pause control
Meta refresh tags that reload the page on a timer with no user control
Form submission deadlines that provide a timer but no extension mechanism
Timeout warnings that appear for only a few seconds and dismiss automatically before the user can respond
Auto-updating content (stock tickers, sports scores) that refreshes the entire page rather than just the dynamic region
Session extension dialogs that are not keyboard accessible, effectively trapping keyboard users when the timeout occurs

examples.pass: A banking site displays a warning 60 seconds before session timeout: 'Your session will expire in 60 seconds. Press any key or click Continue to stay logged in.' The user can extend the session up to 10 times by clicking Continue.
examples.fail: An online exam has a 30-minute timer displayed at the top. When time expires, the form is submitted automatically with no prior warning and no option to extend the time. A user with a motor disability who needs extra time cannot complete the exam.
examples.partial: A session timeout warning appears, but it auto-dismisses after only 10 seconds without user action, and the user is then immediately logged out. The warning exists but does not provide adequate time to respond.

plain_meaning: Whenever the page sets a time limit, the user must be able to turn it off, adjust it to at least 10x the default, or extend it with a simple action after a warning. The intent is that users who need more time (reading, motor, cognitive) are not locked out by arbitrary deadlines.
scope_applies_to: Session timeouts that log the user out or discard data
Form-submission deadlines imposed by the page
Carousels or auto-advancing content that removes readable content after a time
Timed quizzes, surveys, or interactive tests set by the content
Any countdown that, when it expires, changes or removes content or functionality
scope_does_not_apply_to: Real-time events where timing is essential (live auctions, live broadcasts)
Timing that is essential to the activity and cannot be changed without invalidating it
Moving or auto-updating visual content (that is SC 2.2.2)
Session expiration solely for security, when extending would invalidate the security (user must still be warned -- see 2.2.5/2.2.6)
Timing between individual keystrokes (that is SC 2.1.1)
borderline_calibration: If no time limit exists on the page, this SC is N/A.
A session timeout with a warning and an Extend Session button satisfies the extend option.
Essential timing (live auction bidding) is exempt -- do not flag.
A carousel that auto-advances is evaluated under 2.2.2, not here, unless it imposes a true deadline that blocks user action.

Script-level detection of timeout keywords is a weak signal — many third-party scripts (analytics, consent managers, ad platforms) contain "session" and "timeout" in their code without implementing user-facing time limits. Require visual or behavioral evidence before flagging.
auditor_anti_patterns: FALSE POSITIVE: flagging a session timeout based solely on detecting timeout-related keywords ("session", "expire", "auto-logout") in JavaScript source code. Many sites include analytics, cookie-consent, or third-party scripts that contain these keywords without implementing actual session timeouts that affect user interaction. Only flag a timeout if you can see evidence of it firing — a warning dialog, a redirect, a countdown, or observable timing behavior. Script keyword detection alone is insufficient evidence.

FALSE POSITIVE: claiming a carousel auto-advances under 2.2.1 when the carousel behavior belongs to SC 2.2.2 (Pause, Stop, Hide). Only flag carousel timing under 2.2.1 if the carousel imposes a deadline that blocks user action or causes data loss when time expires.

GROUND TRUTH: if the programmatic data reports a "session timeout mechanism detected" but the screenshots and video show no timeout warnings, no countdown, and no redirect, set conformance_level to Not Evaluated with insufficient_evidence_reason explaining that the timeout could not be verified from the available evidence. Do NOT fabricate a failure based on unverified script detection.
